text:
Long-horizon time series forecasting is a critical yet challenging task, often plagued by high computational costs and prediction volatility as the forecasting window increases. To address these limitations, we introduce NHITS (Neural Hierarchical Interpolation for Time Series), a novel architecture that enhances neural forecasting efficiency and accuracy. Our approach incorporates two primary innovations: multi-rate data sampling and hierarchical interpolation. By utilizing sub-sampling layers, the model scales its receptive field while significantly reducing the memory footprint. Through hierarchical interpolation, the model decomposes the input signal into various frequency bands, sequentially assembling the forecast from components at different scales.

We provide a theoretical foundation demonstrating that hierarchical interpolation can effectively approximate infinitely long horizons under smoothness assumptions. Empirically, we evaluate NHITS on six large-scale benchmark datasets from the long-horizon literature. Our results show that NHITS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art Transformer-based architectures, achieving an average accuracy improvement of 20% in multivariate settings. Furthermore, NHITS is significantly more efficient, reducing computational time by up to 50 times compared to existing leading methods. The model provides an interpretable, non-linear decomposition of forecasts, making it a robust and parsimonious solution for large-scale predictive systems.
